item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: It shall be done.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I like this approach you have taken.

Considering that there is a MTF based on exploring dreams (Omicron Rho), I suppose they can also be another means to contact Mel. Also, since the Foundation is preparing for operations as though they know Mel is a dream being, it makes sense to explicitly state that to bridge the logic gap.

Therefore…

"Mel" is currently believed to be an Oneiroi (or an equivalent of which) who is associated with and/or the creator of SCP-2144. No means of establishing communications with "Mel" have been conducted thus far. Proposals to contact "Mel" via SCP-990 and/or Mobile Task Force Omicron Rho ("The Dream Team") are currently under review.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So, both earlier in this thread and in discussion with MrWrong, its come to my attention that "Mel" is considered by the author to be a dream entity similar to SCP-990. In the final footnote they mention having difficulty contacting Mel. Considering how friendly SCP-990 is to Foundation personnel, I could see someone in the Foundation suggesting they ask him about establishing contact. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Interviews with Schmidt's peers and family claim no knowledge of any individual referred to as "Mel". In light of additional data elicited from SCP-2305, "Mel" is currently believed to be associated with and/or the creator of SCP-2144. No means of establishing communications with "Mel" have been conducted thus far.

To this:

Interviews with Schmidt's peers and family claim no knowledge of any individual referred to as "Mel". In light of additional data elicited from SCP-2305, "Mel" is currently believed to be associated with and/or the creator of SCP-2144. No means of establishing communications with "Mel" have been conducted thus far. Proposals to contact "Mel" via SCP-990 are currently under review.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well that was gendercentric of me. I apologize, and edited.

Perhaps this is a disagreement of Not Canon. In my headcanon, it's hard to envsion a head researcher not getting a lot of flak for a statement like that about a ghost cat that farts cyanide. Is there more of this character? Perhaps I just missed the memo and Dr An is the poetic character the Foundation could use.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
His speech is very flowery, and seems to be from a perspective that registers the objects to have rights and feelings, which sounds more in line with the anomalous creator than the Foundation.

Technically and firstly, the character is a female.

Secondly and mainly, despite the Foundation rhetoric that SCPs are not entitled to feelings and rights, it is natural for sentient and sapient beings to feel entitled to those. No account of policy enforcement can fully suppress such base desires, so some level of acknowledgement would be in order.

And I suppose not everyone in the Foundation has to adopt the same drone-like tone at work.

As an aside, if it were the anomalous creator, she would have not just stopped at entitlement to rights and feelings (which would be more of a stance from an external party).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I too wish to register a complaint with the final document. I was analyzing the doctor's name, because I was half expecting to find her middle initial to be M. Her speech is very flowery, and seems to be from a perspective that registers the objects to have rights and feelings, which sounds more in line with the anomalous creator than the Foundation.

Edit - well I feel silly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Werner Heisenberg and Erwin Schrödinger were two totally different guys, and were largely opposed both ideologically and scientifically. In particular Schrödinger's famous feline was a critique of the Copenhagen Interpretation. They also had very different relationships with the Nazis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to upvote this, but the bit about the defibrillator makes me wince too much.

What you have in the article is a Magical Defibrillator, as often seen on TV. In reality, a defibrillator exists to stop the heart so that CPR or a pacemaking device can induce the body to restore a normal heartbeat.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's Heisenberg's Cat! There's no horror in this one at all, but I'm okay with that. Not everything anomalous wants to kill you. I enjoy this for what it is and happily +1. Probably because I'm a cat person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that's your primary issue, it has been amended accordingly. It's still exposition since it needs to explain the change in procedures. However, I've fine-tuned it to be a statement issued to justify a policy change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That addendum at the end by Dr. Hu An seems a bit too… Personal, really. Like the doc was playing Exposition Guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see a problem with "masses about".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be odd to say that it "masses about…". Or I could change that to "its mass is about…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I meant here:

that it weighs about 2 to 5 kg.

Should be mass with those units, yes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From the article itself,

Its mass can be recorded in this state, although measurements are inconsistent and ranges from 1.9 kg up to 5.2 kg.

But semantics aside, let's assume that everything's being measured on Earth.

On a side note, I really hate the weight vs mass business.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't that be "masses 2 to 5 kg" (if weight it would be Newtons, but nobody does that)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What do they show?

What do the scales show now?

The mass of the person on both counts. The "cat" has zero mass throughout. It's only that the person carrying it feels the weight, while external objects (i.e. weighing scale) would not register it as something with mass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2144's mass is unable to be measured; scales regularly register its mass as 0.0 kg. However, personnel who had held SCP-2144 estimate that it weighs about 2 to 5 kg.

I pick the cat up and step on the scales. What do they show?
Next I let go of the cat. What do the scales show now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you've figured it out in the end. Quantum physics is a bit esoteric indeed (It took me till post-JC to appreciate this field of physics), so I admit it's hard to get into the meat.

Every idea has its right to exist.

LOL. My personal ethos, in fact. It's less of Schrodinger's Cat and more of an embodiment of an idea caught between life/existence and death/eradication.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quantum superposition is something I've been looking into for a while now, I'm even writing an SCP on it. Now that I've reread it and actually know what the concept is about, I've gotta say, this is an interesting take on it. Especially this line here:

Every idea has its right to exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's from a museum exhibit, it's supposed to show the relationship between skeletal anatomy and external appearance, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am surprised that someone in RL even conceive such a thing as maybe a sculpture or taxidermy thing.

But on a more serious note, let's leave it at that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was basically a cat with a half skeletal head. Based on a picture something like this:

+ show block
– hide block

And was fairly typical of a new author coldpost otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am afraid I am not at liberty to distribute others' works for comparison. But I can say that the item is always a cat, from start to finish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll confess to being a bit slow to catch it. It wasn't until the acquisition that it clicked. The concept is amazing. There's also a Heisenberg factor: when free, it can be observed in motion, but not measured. In the box, it can be measured, but is no longer in motion.

Is there anywhere the original has been archived? I'm curious to compare, not having read this one originally.

Oh, and a major +1

item.title: Update Notice
item.content:encoded:
Noting here that I've updated on the SCP. To be specific, I've changed Addendum 2144-1 into something more subtle and does not cry out "Schrodinger's Cat" directly (since I suspect everyone would have figured it out on their own. If not, my Authorial posts have spoiled it.).

Furthermore, I do think that the dream diaries might have been a bit too "convenient". The content of the dream diaries still stands, although they are never recorded and only Schmidt and Mel know about it.

I have brought back the collar from the original article, although it is just a normal collar now.

EDIT: And I have also shamelessly put in one of my author avatars into this SCP to introduce her in the meta-narrative of Foundation-verse. It makes sense in-context, since An's job is a containment advisor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you're referring to SCP-2303, I read through it and was slightly perplexed by the way its anomalous features were presented. On that basis I had downvoted it… but it was the interview that kinda got me to switch to neutrality.

Since I was doing this draft while 2303 was up, I decided to go for details on my side.

Also, while 2303-1 is a goat that does not actually exist unless you know of it, 2144 is a cat that actually exists but is not meant to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have not even read the description yet, but I am upvoting because these containment procedures are wonderfully evocative.

Edit: You know, this is not what I was expecting, but there was that "Aha!" moment in there, and I'm not actually sure what I was expecting. Upvote stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't tell you whether I was dismayed or elated when, in the middle of the description, I exclaimed, "It's Schrodinger's cat!" I became more dismayed once the concept was eluded to directly, but then the whole "dream thought birth" thing took this in an entirely different direction, not to mention the fact that they're trying very hard to keep it alive as part of the containment procedures. I think this might actually be better than the psychedelic goat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that's what I got from Wikipedia. But Google Translate was hardly helpful…

But since the entire passage was explicitly stated to be translated from German, it feels odd as to why a single word is left untranslated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looking at your comments, I find most of them valid and altered them accordingly. Thank you for highlighting them.

While observation of the cat is presumably part of the reference, I felt that this was a little longer than it needed to be. Are the transceivers, standby guards and duty schedules necessary?

I can presume that the transceivers are standard issue gear for guards to begin with. However, the point is that it can only be seen by human eyes and this pertains to Special Containment Procedures (whereby it is a specific aspect of the SCP). The standby guards are necessary to ensure direct observation is maintained no matter what. The schedule is a slight add-on for the sake of practicality, to ensure it is indeed watched.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few small comments:

+ Seek
- And you shall find

Once per month, the enclosure's structural integrity is to be inspected by a containment engineer. If any damage to the enclosure is identified, a containment engineer is to repair said damage.

This didn't seem to relate to the cat's anomalous properties - it's not causing particular damage to the enclosure, and any damage won't cause it to release the gas. Wouldn't general structural inspections be part of standard (rather than special) containment procedures?

An observation post is to be established along the perimeter of the enclosure, with transceivers placed in each post. At all times, a guard is to be stationed in the observation post to watch over SCP-2144. An auxiliary group of up to 3 guards are to be put on standby, in case the assigned guard is unavailable for observation duty. Guards on duty are to rotate in shifts of 2 hours apiece; see Document 2144-Ø for observation duty schedule. At the beginning of each shift, the guard on duty is to acknowledge SCP-2144's presence via transceiver.

While observation of the cat is presumably part of the reference, I felt that this was a little longer than it needed to be. Are the transceivers, standby guards and duty schedules necessary?

An observation post … in each post.

Is there one post, or more than one?

weighing scales

I'd suggest just "scales".

personnel who had held SCP-2144 estimates it to weigh

"personnel who have held SCP-2144 estimate that it weighs"

Its heartbeat can only be detected under such a state, along with signs of respiration.

"Its heartbeat and respiration can be detected only while in this state."

Furthermore, SCP-2144's image and sounds can only be photographed and recorded in this state.

"Furthermore, it is only in this state that SCP-2144's image can be photographed." If the cat ceases all movement in "dead" phase, presumably it isn't making sounds?

Its anomalous properties were revealed when it resumes motion when taken out of the residence.

"when it resumed motion after removal from the residence"

Jason Schmidt is capable of lucid dreaming and has a habit to record details of his dreams

Jason is dead, so I suggest "Jason Schmidt was capable of lucid dreaming and habitually recorded details of his dreams"

Summary: this is a nice iterative improvement, keeping the bones of the original idea. A little too much of what feels like filler in the procedures is keeping it from an upvote from my perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In German it is spelled "Heureka".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this intended to be emphatic or is it redundant?

If I must pick one of the above, it would be "emphatic". The point would be to show the contrast when the "cat" is in different environments.

Eureka is Greek, isn't it? Unless you mean it is written with the English spelling

It is indeed "Eureka", as in the English spelling.

EDIT: Since the alphabets for German and English languages are largely similar, there's little point in explicitly stating that it is "in English" since it is supposed written down and not spoken. "Eureka" will still be quoted, to imply that it is one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple of details.

Its mass can also be recorded as well

Is this intended to be emphatic or is it redundant? I'd suggest taking "also" out.

On the board were the words "Eureka", in English.

Eureka is Greek, isn't it? Unless you mean it is written with the English spelling, but I'm uncertain whether it's a language-specific translation.

That said, I know what you're going for, I get it… but I'm afraid I'm not feeling it. Will re-read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone was bound to make this;p

Though, a lot more unique and complex than most would go for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was my plan from the beginning. I figured that the only way one can pull off a literal Schrodinger's Cat is to acknowledge its origins as something based in the world of thoughts and ideas.

As to Josie, who knows? One of my earlier concepts involve the implication that a variant of SCP-2144 will manifest when someone thinks of Schrodinger's Cat literally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this has developed into a Tulpa, neat!

It makes me wonder if Josie isn't also a Tulpa generated by thinking about Schroedinger's cat in a more abstract way (if you never collapse the waveform, do you have a half-cat?).

item.title: Rewrite Attribution and Other Matters
item.content:encoded:
This article is a rewrite of a previous iteration of SCP-2144 by Schrodingers_Kitty (original Author of the work). I would also like to thank the Rewrite Team (chiefly Decibelles, who was my main contact) for the opportunity to rewrite this article.

This is inspired by my experiences in Junior College, where I initially thought Schrodinger's Cat as a literal concept.

So what's going on?

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-2144 is a literal Schrodinger's Cat.

Whatever Mel is, she's the same class of beings as SCP-990. Sentient dream-beings.

SCP-2144 is effectively a thought-form, a "brainchild" of Mel and Jason that is half memetic and half "realised". That was partially inspired by the Percy Jackson series' concept of a "child of Athena" who are effectively "brainchildren".

As to its odd features of its heart rate slowing to potentially death, I would say that it is the fact that it is a flawed idea – it's no meant to be a thing. It's sort of a corrective impulse (or "instinct") to set things right by sending itself towards death.
